content.full_text:
California
Fair Political
Practices Commission

March 10, 1995

Mr. Roy A. Woodward
Woodward and Associates
8781-C Cuyamaca
Santee, California 92071

Re: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-95-049

Dear Mr. Woodward:

You have asked for advice concerning the conflict-of-interest
provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").¹

QUESTION

Will you have potential conflicts of interest under the Act
if you become a member of a City of Santee committee known as the
"Town Center Task Force" when you have an ownership interest in
several properties within and in close proximity to the
redevelopment area about which the task force is to make
recommendations?

CONCLUSION

Based on the facts presented, it appears that the Town Center
Task Force is merely an advisory body that does not have
governmental decisionmaking authority. Thus, if you became a
member of the Task Force, you would not be considered a public
official under the Act and would not be subject to the Act's
conflict-of-interest provisions.

FACTS

You are a realtor in the City of Santee and have applied for
membership on the city's Town Center Task Force. You own or have
an interest in several properties in close proximity to the
redevelopment area as well as two properties within the
redevelopment area itself.

_______________________

¹ Government Code Sections 81000-91015. All statutory references
are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated. Commission
Regulations appear at 2 California Code of Regulations, Sections
18000-18954. All references to regulations are to Title 2,
Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations.

428 J Street, Suite 800 • P.O. Box 807 • Sacramento, CA 95804-0807 • (916) 322-5660

File No. A-95-049
Page 2


You stated that the task force is an advisory body which
provides non-binding recommendations to the Santee City Council
regarding the development of the town center (the redevelopment
area). However, final governmental decisions about the
development of the town center are made by the city council. The
task force has no funding and individuals who serve on it receive
no salary or reimbursement. It is composed of two councilmembers
and several public members.

ANALYSIS

The Act prohibits a public official from making,
participating in making, or in any way attempting to influence a
governmental decision in which the official knows or has reason to
know he or she has a financial interest. (Section 87100.)

The conflict-of-interest provisions of the Act apply only to
"public officials," including "designated employees." (Section
87100.) The term "designated employee" is defined in Section
82019 to specifically exclude the salaried members of boards or
commissions that serve a solely advisory function. Regulation
18700(a)(1) provides guidelines in determining whether a board or
commission is "solely advisory" or has decisionmaking authority.
It provides:

"Public official at any level of state or local
government" means a member, officer, employee, or
consultant of a state or local government agency....

(1) "Member" shall include, but not be limited to,
salaried or unsalaried members of boards or commissions
with decisionmaking authority. A board or commission
possesses decisionmaking authority whenever:

(A) It may make a final governmental decision;

(B) It may compel a governmental decision; or it
may prevent a governmental decision either by reason of
an exclusive power to initiate the decision or by reason
of a veto which may not be overriden; or

(C) It makes substantive recommendations which
are, and over an extended period of time have been,
regularly approved without significant amendment or
modification by another public official or governmental
agency.

Regulation 18700(a)(1).

A board or commission which does not possess decisionmaking
authority pursuant to the provisions of Regulation 18700(a)(1) is
solely advisory in nature. (See In re Rotman (1987) 10 FPPC Ops.
1.) Accordingly, its members are not public officials.

File No. A-95-049
Page 3


     Based on the facts you have provided, it appears that the
task force is a purely advisory body without decisionmaking
authority. The task force does not make any final governmental
decisions, as these are reserved for the council. Further, the
recommendations the task force makes are often subject to
extensive debate and reconsideration by the council. Thus,
membership on the task force would not make you a "public
official" subject to the Act's conflict-of-interest provisions.
Your ownership of properties within the redevelopment area and
membership on the Town Center Task Force would not give rise to a
conflict of interest under the Act.

     If you have any other questions regarding this matter, please
contact me at 916/322-5901.

                                        Sincerely,

                                        Steven G. Churchwell
                                        General Counsel

                                        [signature]
                                        By: Hyla P. Wagner
                                        Counsel, Legal Division

*
